PURPOSE

This manual provides guidance and information to staff tasked with investigating child abuse in Western Australia. It is important that the content of this manual is read in conjunction with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine and not viewed in isolation.

In respect of child sexual offences committed within the Perth metropolitan area, the majority will be dealt with by the Child Abuse Squad (CAS) and the Sexual Assault Squad

(SAS) according to the terms of their Charter and Table of Responsibilities (see Appendix

‘A’). There are some offences, inclusive of physical assaults, which are the responsibility of the metropolitan districts.

In Regional WA the responsibility for the investigation of child sexual offences is reversed in that most cases will be dealt with by Regional Western Australia police. Primacy will only pass to CAS following agreement between the two business units, taking into consideration the seriousness or complexity of an individual case.

Any feedback or recommendations pertaining to this manual should be directed to the Investigative Standards Unit.

SCOPE

This manual relates to offences committed against children as defined in the Western Australia Criminal Code.

The investigation of any offence within this category should be conducted with due consideration to the advice and guidance contained within the Child Abuse Squad Charter (Appendix ‘A’).

INTRODUCTION

All investigations conducted into offences of child abuse will be in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine with particular reference to:

 Appointment of an Investigating Officer (IO) or where required a Senior Investigating Officer (SIO).

 Application of the CRIME Model.

 Formulation of strategies under The5KIS.

 Categorisation of the investigation, and

 Prioritisation of the investigation.

Some investigations will overlap in terms of ownership by the relevant business unit.

Officers should always consult with the Officer in Charge of the relevant business unit(s) for guidance.

sections.h:
During 2015, over 3000 IMS reports were submitted within Western Australia advising that child abuse was involved. In terms of seriousness offences range from relatively minor physical assault through to serious physical, sexual assault and homicide. The seriousness of any offence of child abuse cannot be overstated with the potential for unchecked minor abuse leading to more serious levels of offending. This notion is reflected in the introduction of the mandatory reporting of child sexual abuse in the amendment to the Children and Community Services Act 2004 in January 2009.

Motivation for offending can range from overzealous chastisement of a child through to gratuitous and in some cases organised paedophilia. For these reasons it is important that any suspicions of child abuse are investigated with the same rigour as more overt cases irrespective of the source of the report.

The difficulty in identifying any signs and symptoms of abuse other than the presence of overt physical injury cannot be overstated. No specific behavioural syndromes characterise victims of sexual abuse. Sexual abuse involves a wide range of possible behaviours which appear to have widely varying effects on victims. The absence of any sexualised behaviour does not confirm that sexual abuse did not take place any more than the presence of sexualised behaviour conclusively demonstrates that sexual abuse occurred; rather, both pieces of information affect the level of suspicion concerning the child's possible experiences and should serve to promote careful impartial investigation.

There is a wealth of national and international research identifying the significant impacts of cumulative harm on a child’s wellbeing. In conjunction with this research, child protection service providers acknowledge that child involvement in domestic violence is a significant risk factor in predicting harm to the child. There is also a connection between domestic violence involving children and the escalation of that violence into incidents of serious harm or homicide. As police are often the first point of contact for women and their children involved in domestic violence, they have a significant role to play in preventing violence through positive intervention and effective risk assessment.

The WA Police Force has implemented a policy to refer all children involved in domestic violence to the Department for Child Protection and Family Support (CPFS). This is to ensure information holdings about these at-risk children are centrally stored and able to be accessed and investigated by experts in assessment of child harm.

Child Abuse Squad

The Child Abuse Squad (CAS) is located at 79 Stirling Street Perth and forms part of the Sex Crime Division. In July 2015, the Child Assessment and Interview Team and the Online Child Exploitation Squad combined with CAS to form the one business unit all known as CAS. CAS is divided into several business streams. These are;

 Intake and Assessment Team

 Specialist Child Interviewing Team

 Joint Anti Child Exploitation Team (JACET)

 Investigation Teams

The Intake and Assessment Team, receive, assess and triage all allegations of child sexual abuse within the state of Western Australia. The team is co -located with CPFS Childfirst and together they capture, analyse, share, evaluate and distribute all available information. This multi-agency approach of the WA Police Force and CPFS seeks to promote and safeguard the well-being of children affected by child abuse as well as agreeing on the best way for the investigation to progress.

The Specialist Child Interview Team provides assistance to police operations within the state and throughout Australia. Their primary role is to provide an interview serv ice for all children who are victims of child sexual abuse. The team is staffed with Police Officers and CPFS Childfirst personnel trained to interview children who have experienced or witnessed offences as defined by Schedule 7 of the Evidence Act 1906. The Interview Team is responsible for:

1. Electronically recorded interviews of children in the metropolitan area who have

experienced sexual and/or serious physical abuse as outlined in Schedule 7, Evidence Act 1906.

2. Providing assistance by way of staff and advice to specialist child interviewers and

detectives state-wide.

3. Providing Specialist Child Interviewing and refresher courses state-wide.

The Interview Team can provide an after hour response where available, to investigations, through the on-call supervisor at CAS.

The Joint Anti Child Exploitation Team (JACET) is the co-location of WA Police Force investigators and federal agents from the Australian Federal Police. Together the team investigate on-line sexual grooming offences and child exploitation material offences that are referred from local, national and international sources.

The Investigation Teams are staffed solely by WA Police Force investigators who conduct investigations into contact offences as described within th eir charter of responsibility.

Changes to Reporting Procedures

On 1 January 2009, the legislation that governs mandatory reporting of child sexual abuse became part of the Children and Community Services Act 2004 (Refer Appendix ‘A’). This legislation placed a legal requirement on police officers, doctors, nurses, midwives and teachers to report suspected child sexual abuse to the DCP.

To enable the WA Police Force to ensure that all officers fulfil their obligations as mandatory reporters, the Intake and Assessment Team developed a reporting process to submit reports on behalf of individual officers. Additionally the Officer in Charge and the Operations Managers at CAS were declared as approved persons to receive reports on behalf of CPFS.

Exchange of Information between Prescribed

Public Authorities

Section 28B of the Children and Community Services Act 2004 allows for the exchange of relevant information between prescribed authorities and authorised entities provided the information is relevant to the wellbeing of a child or a class or group of children.

These provisions facilitate effective cooperation between key state government agencies on child protection matters. This power has been delegated to every Police Officer in the WA Police Force.

Below are some examples of Prescribed Authorities:

 WA Heath (Department of Health, Metropolitan Health Services, WA Country Health Service, Peel Health Service).

 The Registrar of the Family Law Court of Western Australia .

 Mental Health Commission.

 WA Police Force.

 Department of Education.

 Department of Education Services.

 Department of Housing.

 Department of Local Government & Communities.

 Department of Corrective Services.

 Department of Attorney General.

 Disability Services Commission.

 Department of Indigenous Affairs.

Statutory Duty to Notify Corruption and Crime

Commission

Section 28 of the Corruption and Crime Commission Act 2003, requires Police to notify the Commission in writing of any matter, where Police suspect on reasonable grounds concerns or may concern misconduct involving a government employee.

sections.h:
The application of the CRIME model focuses on the five key areas of:

1) Contact

2) Respond

3) Investigate

4) Management

5) Evaluation

CONTACT

This function may often be carried out by a police staff member in a call handling or station office role. Guidance contained within the WA Police Force Manual should be used as a reference source.

The initial contact officer (ICO) should advise the person police are there to assist with the matter and should obtain details from the victim (caller) to determine:

 The welfare of the victim.

 Minimise the potential for loss of evidence, and

 The correct resource response to the matter.

When obtaining the information the ICO should give the victim as much privacy as possible and limit questions to the following issues:

 Need for medical assistance and personal safety of the victim.

 Identity, location and description of the suspect (if known).

 Time of the offence in order to prioritise action, and  Location of the incident scene(s).

In relation to the engagement of specialist resources for child sexual matters, the ICO must refer the allegation to CAS who will carry out a risk assessment in line with the four phase prioritisation criteria contained within their Charter. CAS maintain an on-call supervisor after business hours for advice as required. Where there is any doubt a s to the engagement of CAS over child sexual allegations, call handlers should err on the side of caution. It is better to consult CAS at this stage rather than make a decision which can have far reaching consequences later in the investigation.

RESPOND

A commonality with the majority of incidents is that the initial response will be provided by district personnel. However there may be cases where owing to the circumstances of the child sexual abuse allegation, more discretion is required when determinin g the style of response. For example where the alleged offender is a family member and where the child is not in immediate danger it may be more appropriate to delay the mode of response in negotiation with the person reporting. Recourse to an overt unifor med response should only be made after consideration of all of the known facts surrounding the case.

In all cases of child sexual abuse allegations, the ICO must advise the on -call member of CAS of the allegation. The CAS on-call telephone number is published on Outlook.

When a report is received by the Intake and Assessment Team, the circumstances must be subject to a risk assessment and priority allocation from the Intake and Assessment supervisor.

In terms of the level of response at the outset, the priority is the welfare of the child. The level of response must be in line with the associated risks to the victim in line with Priority 1-4 contained within the Child Abuse Squad Charter (Appendix ‘A’).

Multi-Agency Strategy Meetings

A strategy meeting is arranged between CPFS and WA Police Force with other stakeholders invited as required. This meeting will discuss actions and participants reach agreement on the way to proceed. Meetings are usually attended by representatives from CPFS and Police. Representatives from Education, Health, and other agencies may participate appropriate to the individual circumstances of the case. The decisions made during the strategy meeting/discussion should be clearly documented.

NB: The time lapse from the first report to intake and assessment to the meeting should be no more than 24 hours.

What are the objectives of the strategy meeting?

Clarity about the objectives is important to:

 Ensure effective multi-agency work by involving people from key agencies.

 Facilitate the lawful exchange of information between agencies.

 Reach a shared view about how serious the alleged abuse may be.

 Establish a common understanding about the overall breadth of the investigation required

 Agree on tasks and timings for each stage of the investigation.

 Identify and implement a safety plan to protect any child pending the outcome of an assessment.

Content of the Strategy Meeting

The following issues will need to be considered at a strategy meeting:

 What are the wishes, if known, of the child involved?

 Is there a need to consider the continuing safety of the child? For example, is there a need for immediate protective action either on a voluntary basis or through the courts?

 Is it possible there are other victims?

 How can family or carers be involved? Who should be interviewed, what is the best place and time for interviews?

 The need to preserve evidence.

 In criminal investigations, the child should be considered for an evidentiary interview (Visually Recorded Interview) or statement in accordance with the procedures.

 How can information about the child best be gathered?

 Is there a need for a medical examination?

 When and how should the child and/or their carers be involved in relevant meetings? How does their current level of distress affect their involvement?

Should they be present at meetings or are there other ways they can contribute to decision making?

 What practical assistance would facilitate the child’s involvement and co - operation; for example, transport to a clinic and interviews, assistance with childcare arrangements, interpreter?

 What personal support do families need; for example, links with support groups, separate workers for different family members?

 What arrangements should be made to facilitate the involvement and contribution of children with disabilities; for example, conducting interviews in buildings with easy access, the use of interpreters and specialist staff?

 Are there issues of race, culture, language or gender that require special arrangements to be made?

 Should a worker known to the child/adult/carers/family undertake the Child Assessment Interview, or should a ‘neutral’ worker do this and should tasks be shared by more than one worker?

NB: Consideration will need to be given to when and how the alleged perpetrator is made aware of the allegations. Consideration will also need to be given as to who is best placed to notify them of this investigation.

Regional Investigations

Regional WA police districts are responsible for the investigation and manageme nt of child abuse offences within their respective districts. At times, depending on the complexity of an investigation, it may be necessary to request the assistance of CAS.

Deployment of CAS to Regional WA can be initiated by consultation with the OIC CA S or the Sex Crime Divisional Office. The regional district requesting the assistance may be required to submit a tasking request through the Sex Crime Division (SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG).

Investigating Officer

The officer appointed to this role has overall responsibility for the investigative approach and should be engaged as soon as practicable following the initial disclosure. Their role includes:

 Management of the investigation in accordance with the CRIME model and application of The5KIS.

 The completion of immediate actions including:

 Ensuring the victim’s medical needs are met.

 Assessing the account from the victim to determine investigative strategies.

 Assessing the scenes (including the victim, location(s) and suspect).

 Ensure preservation of forensic evidence where subsequent examination is required.

 Identifying any witnesses or methods of tracing witnesses.

 Arrest suspect(s) on suspicion, if required.

 Ensure accurate records of potential evidentiary issues (e.g. first complaint).

 Ensure accurate records are maintained regarding investigative actions and decisions.

INVESTIGATE

All investigations will be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine with strict adherence to the CRIME model and THE5KIS listed below:

 Physical Material

 Witnesses

 Intelligence

 Public Awareness

 Suspect

Regional Investigations Regional WA police districts are responsible for the investigation and manageme nt of child abuse offences within their respective districts. At times, depending on the complexity of an investigation, it may be necessary to request the assistance of CAS. Deployment of CAS to Regional WA can be initiated by consultation with the OIC CA S or the Sex Crime Divisional Office. The regional district requesting the assistance may be required to submit a tasking request through the Sex Crime Division (SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG). Investigating Officer The officer appointed to this role has overall responsibility for the investigative approach and should be engaged as soon as practicable following the initial disclosure. Their role includes:  Management of the investigation in accordance with the CRIME model and application of The5KIS.  The completion of immediate actions including:  Ensuring the victim’s medical needs are met.  Assessing the account from the victim to determine investigative strategies.  Assessing the scenes (including the victim, location(s) and suspect).  Ensure preservation of forensic evidence where subsequent examination is required.  Identifying any witnesses or methods of tracing witnesses.  Arrest suspect(s) on suspicion, if required.  Ensure accurate records of potential evidentiary issues (e.g. first complaint).  Ensure accurate records are maintained regarding investigative actions and decisions. INVESTIGATE All investigations will be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine with strict adherence to the CRIME model and THE5KIS listed below:  Physical Material  Witnesses  Intelligence  Public Awareness  Suspect

sections.h:
The construction of a forensic strategy is a key priority for the IO in the early stages of the investigation into offences of child abuse. In most incidents of child abuse it is likely that, at some point, legitimate access to the victim or their home has been gained by a suspect, and that the suspect may be living with, or has lived with, the victim as a parent or carer, relative or visitor. This presents different issues from those where the suspect is unidentified or has no legitimate access to the scene. In cases where the suspect has had legitimate access, it will not usually be sufficient to identify the suspect as being present at the scene from forensic evidence.

The forensic investigation should consider the sequence of events as explained by each party, looking at the scene in that context. This means testing whether the information from the scene corroborates the sequence of events supplied by the suspect as well as the victims and witnesses. This principle may also apply to scenes of previous incidents.

Potential areas of significant evidence which could assist the investigator include:

 Blood traces

 Fingerprints

 Medication

 Clothing and bedding

 Footmarks

 Injuries

 Weapons

 Obvious signs of neglect/abuse

On arrival at the scene and/or place of reporting it is important to note the demeanour of the complainant, particularly in those cases where the person reporting is the victim.

Initial interaction with the person reporting should focus on the twin imperatives of:

 Victim safety.

 Securing and preservation of evidence in accordance with The5KIS.

Opportunities to obtain physical material at this stage may be limited but could include:

 Photographs of any obvious injuries.

 Consideration of use of Preliminary Evidence Kit in sexual abuse cases.

 Description of physical appearance and condition of the victim.

 Observations of living conditions.

 Access to computer equipment and internet particularly in sexual abuse cases.

 Medical history of the victim.

With regard to the physical examination of the victim, early consideration must be given to the need for a specially trained doctor and/or paediatrician to be engaged so the appropriate skills are made available to investigators.

Princess Margaret Hospital Child Protection Unit

Princess Margaret Hospital Child Protection Unit (PMH CPU) carries out assessments of children when there is a concern they have, or may have, suffered from abuse. PMH CPU provides medical, forensic, social work and therapeutic services, along with training and education of other professionals working with children. The CPU is staffed by paediatricians, senior social workers and therapists.

Medical Service

 Medical examination and opinion.

 Diagnosis and treatment of injuries and infections.

 Medical investigations, including blood tests and imaging.

 Medical reports.

Forensic Medical Service

 A forensic medical examination and opinion.

 The doctor may complete forensic medical investigations.

 The doctor may compile a medical report for legal proceedings.

Social Work Service

 An assessment regarding the child’s current and ongoing safety.

 A psycho-social assessment.

 Crisis support and assistance for the child and their family.

 Referral to therapeutic services.

Therapy Service

 Offers a range of therapy services to children, adolescents and their families where abuse has occurred.

 Therapy services include individual, group and family therapy.

WITNESSES

In intra familial child abuse cases witnesses may emanate from a number of sources including family members, friends and carers. In sexual abuse cases it must be noted that a witness might also be the person providing evidence of early complaint for evidential purposes. All relevant procedures for the securing of witness evidence including any special legal provisions must be observed.

All witness interviews must be conducted in accordance with the WA Police Investigative Interviewing Regime and by officers trained to the relevant standard.

Evidentiary Child Interview

The below objectives underpin the WA Police Force joint response with CPFS in conducting an assessment of a child’s environment and suitability to subjecting the child to an Evidentiary Child Interview. The joint approach seeks to:

 Provide a standard systematic approach in conducting interviews with children who have experienced sexual and/or physical abuse.

 Provide a framework within which to conduct interviews.

 Provide standards of qualifications of Specialist Child Interviews in accordance with Section 106HA of the Evidence Act.

 Provide a state-wide quality assurance process of visually recorded child interviews.

 Enhance the professionalism within which interviews are conducted.

 Provide integrity to interviews that may come under judicial scrutiny.

 Promote accountability of interviews.

 Promote confidence in members attached to the unit when confronted with complex, high profile interviews.

Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)

Officers are reminded that victims may be suffering from the effects of Post Traumatic Stress Disorder which may affect their ability/willingness to recall exact details of the abuse. For this reason the securing of victim testimony may be necessary over an extended period of time.

It is long established practice, the obtaining of victim testimony must be carried out by trained Specialist Child Interviewers for offences within Schedule 7 of the Evidence Act 1906.

Early Complaint Evidence

In some child abuse cases there will be an element of sexual abuse. In such cases it is necessary for officers to be cognisant of the need to address the notion of ‘Early Complaint’. Evidence of early complaint is an exception to the rules of hear say which relates to the first person(s) the victim informed as to the nature of the offence. This can be soon after the assault or as soon as practicable thereafter. In practice the disclosure of the assault can be sometime after the event, in some cases a number of years. More commonly the early complaint is made relatively soon after the assault.

In evidential terms it is important to secure this disclosure as research suggests the content of the disclosure is often of significant evidential value for t he furtherance of the investigation.

When obtaining evidence of early complaint officers should record the following information from the witness:

 The circumstances in which disclosure took place including location and method i.e. in person/by telephone.

 Victim’s disclosure – verbatim.

 Any witnesses to the first complaint.

 The victim’s demeanour. Include the state of dress, physical injuries or conditions such as crying or hysteria; and

 If the report was made to POC or the 000 service an extract of the cal l should be secured. The officer receiving the call could give evidence of early complaint and a statement should be taken from the officer as soon as practicable.

Where the victim has related the circumstances of the assault to a number of different people and there is confusion as to which person is the first complaint witness, interviews should be conducted with those persons and statements obtained. In the event of a prosecution the ODPP should be consulted in order to establish which person is the early complaint witness in law.

General Witnesses

Witnesses in this category can include those who can provide testimony for continuity, background, intelligence and alibi.

In all cases the requirement to show continuity in exhibit handling is an import ant feature.

This is particularly so in sex assault cases where the potential for cross contamination is heightened owing to the nature of the evidence.

An additional feature of these assaults is the requirement to confirm the alleged offender had the opportunity to commit the crime in the sense there is no evidence to suggest the suspect was in a different place at the time of the assault. Where a suspect gives notice of alibi, investigators must go to great lengths to ensure the alibi provided is thoroug hly investigated before a suspect can be eliminated from the enquiry.

Expert Witnesses

An additional feature of child abuse investigations is the use of expert witnesses such as doctors, forensic scientists and psychologists to interpret evidential findin gs. In all cases involving their employment cognisance should be taken of the experience and qualifications when formulating a statement of evidence.

INTELLIGENCE

Developing strategies to obtain relevant sources of information include the following;

 Entering relevant intelligence on the IMS.

 In cases where the identity of the offender is unknown cross check intelligence data bases in the areas of:

 crime profile of the area

 any precursor offences

 known offenders in the area

 linked crimes.

Contact should be made with your local district or divisional intelligence section for the allocation of an analyst to assist with intelligence related investigative actions.

SUSPECTS

The investigative strategies used for a child abuse offence suspect should be well planned and demonstrate an awareness of the potential motivations, the need to avoid contamination/loss of physical material and or the potential of putting the victim or witnesses at risk.

It is important for investigating officers to build a profile of suspect behaviour leading to and following the offence. Areas to cover with the suspect include:

 Circumstances leading up to the alleged offence.

 Motive when committing the alleged offence.

 Character of the suspect.

 Previous child abuse by the suspect.

 General relationships with children and child contact arrangements, if applicable.

 Nature of other relationships, past and present, including history of abuse and controlling.

 Behaviour on the part of the suspect (e.g., domestic violence).

 Relationship with other family members, family structure and functioning.

 Any circumstances where children of the family lived away from home.

 Contact with external support agencies.

 Parenting style, where applicable.

 Self-care and care of other family members, where applicable.

 Significant family events, such as deaths and illnesses.

 Issues relating to grooming such as membership of children’s organisations and fostering of interests in children’s websites.

 Other abuse towards strangers and acquaintances.

 Any drug or alcohol misuse issues.

Interview of a Suspect

All suspect interviews must be carried out within the framework of the PEACE model.

Attention should be paid to planning and preparing for a suspect interview.

Where there is no direct evidence importance should be attached to corroborating the account of the victim. For example placing the complainant and suspect together at particular places, on particular times or dates. The same importance should be attached to any age issues where the nature of the charge is predicated on the age of the victim.

For example, the suspect should be asked to provide an explanation as to why he believed the victim was of a certain age.

Bail Considerations

In child abuse investigations there is often some form of relationship between the suspect and victim. Many offences take place in the home and between parties who are co - habiting. For these reasons it is important to emphasise the importance of ensuring post charge contact between the two parties and key witnesses is prevented by appropriate imposition of bail conditions.

In reaching a decision on the application for bail conditions investigators should consider the following influencing factors:

 Associated risks to the victim or future victims.

 Protective conditions.

 Surety bail.

 Violence Restraining Orders.

sections.h:
Investigating Officers are reminded of the need for confidentiality in most cases of child abuse so the anonymity of relevant parties is protected. Off icers should use the WA Police Investigation Doctrine as a reference source for potential strategies.

MANAGEMENT

There is always the potential for child abuse cases to involve an element of risk to the victim or future victims as well as the loss of physical evidence. It is important therefore in managing the investigation, consideration is given to the following:

 Timeliness of the investigation and ensuring immediate response where required, and

 Managing the associated risks to the victim and to the agency.

The allocation of a child abuse investigation must be in accordance with the WA Police Investigation Doctrine and the Child Abuse Squad Charter (see Appendix ‘A’. It is essential a CRIME file is commenced in all circumstances and an Investigation Action Register maintained to assist with the management of the investigation.

There may be a requirement to engage with other departments to ensure awareness of the investigation is raised in a wider arena minimising associated risks. This include s other departments within WA Police Force but also external partners such as CPFS, PMH CPU and victim support agencies (see section on Follow up Victim Services).

Child Witness Services

The Child Witness Service helps children and young people under 18 years of age who may need to give evidence in court. The children involved can be victims or witnesses to any criminal charge, in any court. Practical information is provided to help children and young people emotionally prepare for court. Support is provided o n an individual basis.

Evidence is never discussed.

It is important children are referred to the Child Witness Service as soon as possible after charges have been laid against an alleged offender. It is not necessary to know for certain if the child is required to give evidence. Child victims and witnesses can receive support from the time charges are laid until when the matter is finalised in court.

Victim Support Services

Counsellors at the PMH CPU provide counselling support to children affected by p revious child abuse violence. People can be referred for ongoing individual or group counselling following an emergency service. They can also be referred by other age ncies or through self-referral.

The Victim Support Service (VSS) will also provide assistance to victims of crime and in particular to victims of violent personal crime. The IO following contact with the victim’s parent/guardian should advise the VSS of the details of the victim and the circumstances surrounding the incident including details of any person charged.

This needs to be done with the consent of the victim’s parent/guardian. Once advised the VSS will make contact with the victim and offer support and services. Contact should be made with the VSS as soon as possible after the incide nt to allow that agency to provide the appropriate support.

EVALUATE

Case Supervision

Maintaining victim confidence is a key factor in sex offence investigations. The trauma and anxiety intrinsic in the victims experience can be aggravated in cases which, to them, appear to be long and drawn out, with the potential to add to the angst.

It is preferable therefore, all investigations into sex offences are conducted in a timely manner which maintains victim confidence and also ensures the matter has be en subject to a thorough investigation. There is a requirement for supervisors to monitor the pace and the effectiveness of the investigation so these two imperative s are delivered where possible.

All incidents of Child Abuse related offences will be subject to evaluation under the auspices of the WA Police Investigation Doctrine in relation to its resolution, any endorsement to charge and subsequent review process.

Case Review

The reviewing of all cases will be in accordance with the WA Police Crime Review Policy.

Case Review The reviewing of all cases will be in accordance with the WA Police Crime Review Policy.

sections.h:
ODPP Liaison

The Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) is the independent prosecuting authority for the State of Western Australia and has appointed a liaison officer for WA Police Force. The purpose of this is twofold, firstly to obtain early legal advice in principle as to whether or not a prosecution case exists and secondly, what evidence is needed to further enhance the prospect of a successful prosecution.

Offender’s Antecedents

Once an offender is charged and the matter is referred to the ODPP, any previous convictions of a similar nature, or allegations (particularly those that have not resulted in charges), should be brought to the attention of the ODPP file manager as it may be possible to adduce this as propensity evidence under s.31A of the Evidence Act 1906 (WA).

Particularisation

The concept of particularisation arises from the 1989 High Court of Australia decision Shaw v R (89 ALR 321). This concept is particularly relevant with respect to child sex offences. The following points are of particular relevance:

 The law requires there be certainty as to the particular offence with which a person is charged.

 If an offence is not particularised, then admissibility of similar fact evidence cannot be adequately dealt with.

 It is unfair for the accused to have to defend himself / herself in relation to an indeterminate number of occasions, any one of which may constitute the off ence for which he/she stands charged, if the offences are not particularised. For example, the defence of alibi would be almost impossible to raise.

 A jury, faced with offences that are not particularised, could reach a verdict with some jurors convicting / acquitting in relation to one instance whilst other jurors were convicting / acquitting in relation to a different instance.

A prosecution witness must be able to give evidence about a particular occasion from his / her memory and not rely on vague recollections of offences occurring regularly without detail. The witness must be able to specifically recall the event which constitutes the offence/s and be able to distinguish that event from other similar events.

Corroboration

The concept of corroboration arises from the decision in R v Clynes (1960) 44 Cr App R

1558. Corroboration is independent evidence of some fact that confirms the commission

of the offence and the identity of the accused as being the person who committed the offence.

As a matter of investigative prudence the IO should ensure all corroborative evidence is collected. Corroboration can be found in a number of areas, the list provided is not exhaustive but are suggested areas for consideration:

 House Plans

 Vehicle Registration Records

 Personal diaries

 Medical evidence

 Department of child protection case files

 Previous criminal convictions

 Witness accounts

APPENDIX A – CHARTER OF RESPONSIBILITY

WA Police Force Mission

The Sex Crime Division is part of the State Crime Portfolio of the Western Australia Police Force. The portfolio exists to contribute to the mission:

To provide trusted and valued policing for Western Australia.

Charter of Responsibility

The Child Abuse Squad (CAS) is staffed with investigators but does not have the capacity to investigate every crime committed against a child. It is therefore important for the Squad to investigate the most appropriate offences which are those of a serious, sensitive and/or protracted nature. In order to rationalise and properly manage the correct allocation of investigations a Charter of Responsibility has been developed.

CAS has responsibility for investigating child abuse incidents which fall within the CAS Charter of Responsibility. Child abuse offences falling outside the CAS Charter of Responsibility will be allocated by the Intake and Assessment Team to the CICU of the District in which the victim resides.

Definitions

For the purpose of this Charter, the following definitions will apply:

Interfamilial Where the offender has a familial relationship with the child, either by blood, marriage or other familial connection. Not restricted to lineal relative as defined in Chapter XXXI of the Criminal Code.

Extrafamilial Where the offender does not have a familial relationship with the child, either by blood, marriage or other familial connection.

Serious Injury An injury that constitutes grievous bodily harm or any injury that was incurred as a result of an assault that could have

endangered life (e.g. shaking, drowning etc.)

Criminal Neglect Any person who has the care or control of a child and who engages in conduct causing a detrimental effect of a significant nature on the child’s wellbeing.

Person in Authority A person who has care, supervision or authority of a child for the purposes of Chapter XXXI of the Criminal Code.

Metropolitan WA Charter

The following child abuse investigations, in the metropolitan area, are to be investigated and managed by CAS:

 Sexual abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting.

 Sexual abuse of a child under 13 years of age in an extra familial setting where the offender is known.

 Physical abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting which results in serious injury.

 Physical abuse or sexual abuse of all children within the care of the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of the Department of Child Protection and Family Support (CPFS) where the offender is directly associated or linked to CPFS.

 Sexual abuse or physical abuse of a child where the alleged offender is a person in authority.

 Sexual or physical abuse of a child where the investigation has commenced via a Serious Incident Planning Meeting (SIPM) at Princess Margaret Hospital.

 Criminal neglect incidents of all children under 13 years of age.

 Incidents of such nature as deemed by the Officer in Charge – CAS Other types of child abuse investigations remain the responsibility of the Metropolitan Police District in which the complainant resides. These matters include, but are not limited to:

 Non-serious physical abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting. For example, sibling assaults, unlawful chastisement, etc.

 Sexual abuse of a child 13 years and over in an extra-familial setting. For example indecent assault by a friend/associate where the offender is not a family relation.

 Serious and non-serious physical abuse of all children in an extra-familial setting. For example peer assaults, bullying or assaults by street gangs.

 Historical allegations made by an adult of offences committed upon them while they were a child.

Notwithstanding any other instruction or direction in this Charter, by agreement between CAS and the relevant Metropolitan Police District, reciprocal allocation of any child abuse investigation can occur.

Regional Charter

Regional Police Districts retain responsibility for investigating and managing child abuse offences within their districts. On occasion, due to the complexity of an investigation, it may be necessary to seek the assistance of CAS. The costs of any deployment to Regional WA will be met by CAS.

Deployment of CAS can be initiated by the Sex Crime Division (SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG) via submission of a Tasking Request Form (TRF). Out of sessions applications can be submitted verbally through the Sex Crime Division Divisional office or in consultation with the CAS OIC.

Child Abuse Squad – On Call Supervisor

CAS provides an on-call service through direct contact with a Detective Supervisor on 0421 617 141. This officer provides a single point of contact for police and other agencies in relation to child abuse situations arising outside normal working hours.

The on-call supervisor will respond to calls as quickly as possible, prioritising them according to the information available at the time relating to the degree of vulnerability and risk to safety, with injury or further abuse, and dangers to the victim or other children being the highest priorities. The on-call supervisor will assess information provided and decide if the situation requires immediate attendance of a CAS team, or alternative action. Those callers whose incidents do not require immediate attention will be offered appropriate advice and information. The on-call officer will keep a written record of all calls and actions taken and refer appropriately to the CAIT.

Mandatory Reporting

Section 124B of the Children and Community Services Act (2004) set out that:

1) A person who —

(a) is a doctor, nurse, midwife, police officer or teacher; and

(b) believes on reasonable grounds that a child —

(i) has been the subject of sexual abuse that occurred on or after

commencement day; or

(ii) is the subject of ongoing sexual abuse and;

(c) forms the belief —

(i) in the course of the person’s work (whether paid or unpaid) as a doctor,

nurse, midwife, police officer or teacher; and

(ii) on or after commencement day, must report the belief as soon as

practicable after forming the belief.

This legislation places a legal requirement on police officers, doctors, nurses, midwives and teachers to report suspected child sexual abuse to the DCP who in turn are required to notify WA Police Force to enable a criminal investigation to be conducted. To enable individual police members to comply with this legislation CAIT maintains a Mandatory Reporting process in conjunction with DCP.

Where any police member forms a belief on reasonable grounds that a child is, or has been, sexually abused, the member is required to submit an Incident Management System (IMS) Incident Report detailing the suspected offence. CAIT staff will review all related IMS reports and submit a Mandatory report to DCP on behalf of the submitting officer.

The penalty for failing to adhere to this section of legislation is a fine of up to $6 000.

Risk Assessment and Priority Allocation

On receipt of a reported offence or incident involving child sexual abuse, the Intake and Assessment Team will conduct a risk analysis of that report and assign a priority to the investigation based upon the following Risk Assessment and Priority Allocation protocols:
sections.b: ODPP Liaison The Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) is the independent prosecuting authority for the State of Western Australia and has appointed a liaison officer for WA Police Force. The purpose of this is twofold, firstly to obtain early legal advice in principle as to whether or not a prosecution case exists and secondly, what evidence is needed to further enhance the prospect of a successful prosecution. Offender’s Antecedents Once an offender is charged and the matter is referred to the ODPP, any previous convictions of a similar nature, or allegations (particularly those that have not resulted in charges), should be brought to the attention of the ODPP file manager as it may be possible to adduce this as propensity evidence under s.31A of the Evidence Act 1906 (WA). Particularisation The concept of particularisation arises from the 1989 High Court of Australia decision Shaw v R (89 ALR 321). This concept is particularly relevant with respect to child sex offences. The following points are of particular relevance:  The law requires there be certainty as to the particular offence with which a person is charged.  If an offence is not particularised, then admissibility of similar fact evidence cannot be adequately dealt with.  It is unfair for the accused to have to defend himself / herself in relation to an indeterminate number of occasions, any one of which may constitute the off ence for which he/she stands charged, if the offences are not particularised. For example, the defence of alibi would be almost impossible to raise.  A jury, faced with offences that are not particularised, could reach a verdict with some jurors convicting / acquitting in relation to one instance whilst other jurors were convicting / acquitting in relation to a different instance. A prosecution witness must be able to give evidence about a particular occasion from his / her memory and not rely on vague recollections of offences occurring regularly without detail. The witness must be able to specifically recall the event which constitutes the offence/s and be able to distinguish that event from other similar events. Corroboration The concept of corroboration arises from the decision in R v Clynes (1960) 44 Cr App R 1558. Corroboration is independent evidence of some fact that confirms the commission of the offence and the identity of the accused as being the person who committed the offence. As a matter of investigative prudence the IO should ensure all corroborative evidence is collected. Corroboration can be found in a number of areas, the list provided is not exhaustive but are suggested areas for consideration:  House Plans  Vehicle Registration Records  Personal diaries  Medical evidence  Department of child protection case files  Previous criminal convictions  Witness accounts APPENDIX A – CHARTER OF RESPONSIBILITY WA Police Force Mission The Sex Crime Division is part of the State Crime Portfolio of the Western Australia Police Force. The portfolio exists to contribute to the mission: To provide trusted and valued policing for Western Australia. Charter of Responsibility The Child Abuse Squad (CAS) is staffed with investigators but does not have the capacity to investigate every crime committed against a child. It is therefore important for the Squad to investigate the most appropriate offences which are those of a serious, sensitive and/or protracted nature. In order to rationalise and properly manage the correct allocation of investigations a Charter of Responsibility has been developed. CAS has responsibility for investigating child abuse incidents which fall within the CAS Charter of Responsibility. Child abuse offences falling outside the CAS Charter of Responsibility will be allocated by the Intake and Assessment Team to the CICU of the District in which the victim resides. Definitions For the purpose of this Charter, the following definitions will apply: Interfamilial Where the offender has a familial relationship with the child, either by blood, marriage or other familial connection. Not restricted to lineal relative as defined in Chapter XXXI of the Criminal Code. Extrafamilial Where the offender does not have a familial relationship with the child, either by blood, marriage or other familial connection. Serious Injury An injury that constitutes grievous bodily harm or any injury that was incurred as a result of an assault that could have endangered life (e.g. shaking, drowning etc.) Criminal Neglect Any person who has the care or control of a child and who engages in conduct causing a detrimental effect of a significant nature on the child’s wellbeing. Person in Authority A person who has care, supervision or authority of a child for the purposes of Chapter XXXI of the Criminal Code. Metropolitan WA Charter The following child abuse investigations, in the metropolitan area, are to be investigated and managed by CAS:  Sexual abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting.  Sexual abuse of a child under 13 years of age in an extra familial setting where the offender is known.  Physical abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting which results in serious injury.  Physical abuse or sexual abuse of all children within the care of the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of the Department of Child Protection and Family Support (CPFS) where the offender is directly associated or linked to CPFS.  Sexual abuse or physical abuse of a child where the alleged offender is a person in authority.  Sexual or physical abuse of a child where the investigation has commenced via a Serious Incident Planning Meeting (SIPM) at Princess Margaret Hospital.  Criminal neglect incidents of all children under 13 years of age.  Incidents of such nature as deemed by the Officer in Charge – CAS Other types of child abuse investigations remain the responsibility of the Metropolitan Police District in which the complainant resides. These matters include, but are not limited to:  Non-serious physical abuse of all children in an intra-familial setting. For example, sibling assaults, unlawful chastisement, etc.  Sexual abuse of a child 13 years and over in an extra-familial setting. For example indecent assault by a friend/associate where the offender is not a family relation.  Serious and non-serious physical abuse of all children in an extra-familial setting. For example peer assaults, bullying or assaults by street gangs.  Historical allegations made by an adult of offences committed upon them while they were a child. Notwithstanding any other instruction or direction in this Charter, by agreement between CAS and the relevant Metropolitan Police District, reciprocal allocation of any child abuse investigation can occur. Regional Charter Regional Police Districts retain responsibility for investigating and managing child abuse offences within their districts. On occasion, due to the complexity of an investigation, it may be necessary to seek the assistance of CAS. The costs of any deployment to Regional WA will be met by CAS. Deployment of CAS can be initiated by the Sex Crime Division (SCD) Tasking and Coordination Group (TCG) via submission of a Tasking Request Form (TRF). Out of sessions applications can be submitted verbally through the Sex Crime Division Divisional office or in consultation with the CAS OIC. The on-call supervisor will respond to calls as quickly as possible, prioritising them according to the information available at the time relating to the degree of vulnerability and risk to safety, with injury or further abuse, and dangers to the victim or other children being the highest priorities. The on-call supervisor will assess information provided and decide if the situation requires immediate attendance of a CAS team, or alternative action. Those callers whose incidents do not require immediate attention will be offered appropriate advice and information. The on-call officer will keep a written record of all calls and actions taken and refer appropriately to the CAIT. The penalty for failing to adhere to this section of legislation is a fine of up to $6 000. Risk Assessment and Priority Allocation On receipt of a reported offence or incident involving child sexual abuse, the Intake and Assessment Team will conduct a risk analysis of that report and assign a priority to the investigation based upon the following Risk Assessment and Priority Allocation protocols:

sections.h:
 Child is in immediate danger of being abused by the alleged perpetrator.

 Child other than an identified victim is in immediate danger of being abused by the alleged perpetrator.

 Suspect is a Person in Authority.

 Suspect is a person of high profile.

 Any other circumstances that dictate an immediate response.

 An alleged perpetrator is intending to flee from the state to avoid interview or apprehension. Child victim is a Child in the Care of the CEO DCP.

 Immediate action is required to avoid the loss of evidentiary matter.
sections.b:  Child is in immediate danger of being abused by the alleged perpetrator.  Child other than an identified victim is in immediate danger of being abused by the alleged perpetrator.  Suspect is a Person in Authority.  Suspect is a person of high profile.  Any other circumstances that dictate an immediate response.  An alleged perpetrator is intending to flee from the state to avoid interview or apprehension. Child victim is a Child in the Care of the CEO DCP.  Immediate action is required to avoid the loss of evidentiary matter.

sections.h:
 Child must be removed from potential danger within a time frame, i.e. an upcoming access visit etc.

 Suspect is registered with the Sex Offender Management Squad.

 Suspect is believed to be in possession of CEM and lives with children.

Suspect is believed to be in possession of CEM and has a current Working With Children Card.
sections.b:  Child must be removed from potential danger within a time frame, i.e. an upcoming access visit etc.  Suspect is registered with the Sex Offender Management Squad.  Suspect is believed to be in possession of CEM and lives with children. Suspect is believed to be in possession of CEM and has a current Working With Children Card.

sections.h:
 A child is in no danger of being subjected to further abuse in the short term or the long term.

 No extenuating circumstances are present giving rise of concern for a child’s safety or wellbeing.

 Child exploitation material files where the suspect has a history of CEM / sex offences.
sections.b:  A child is in no danger of being subjected to further abuse in the short term or the long term.  No extenuating circumstances are present giving rise of concern for a child’s safety or wellbeing.  Child exploitation material files where the suspect has a history of CEM / sex offences.

sections.h:
 Historical allegations made by an adult of offences committed upon them as a child.

 Child exploitation material files where there is no other intelligence holding suggesting a child is at imminent risk.

* During a strategy meeting, the allocated priority may be changed in line with each matter on a case by case basis.

Strategy Meeting

CPFS/Police Strategy Meeting - This provides a record of the decisions of a strategy discussion/meeting. In some situations, it may be appropriate for a strategy meeting/discussion to consider all children/young people living in a household.
